headline: Malaysia's Diabetes Rate Dipped in 2023; the Obesity Driving It Kept Climbing
context: The 2023 National Health and Morbidity Survey put adult diabetes at 15.6%, down from 18.3% in 2019, and coverage read it as relief. The same survey's share of adults overweight or obese rose again to 54.4%, the fourth straight increase since 2011.

cards.big: Adult diabetes fell to 15.6% in 2023, down from 18.3% in 2019, and headlines called it the epidemic easing.
cards.sub: One survey down does not make a trend. The number that sets tomorrow's diabetes moved the other way.

cards.big: Overweight and obesity climbed to 54.4% of adults in 2023, up from 50.1% (2019), 47.7% (2015) and 44.5% (2011).
cards.sub: More than one in two adults, about 11 million people, now carry excess weight, the strongest driver of type 2 diabetes.

cards.big: Two in five Malaysians with diabetes do not know they have it. Among those aged 18 to 29, about 84% are unaware.
cards.sub: The headline rate counts the diagnosed. The real burden hides in the undiagnosed, and it skews young.

cards.big: One in two adults lead sedentary lives, and about 84% take part in no sport, fitness or leisure activity.
cards.sub: Physical inactivity is the modifiable input feeding the weight curve, and it sits upstream of every clinic visit.

cards.big: Diabetes is the lagging indicator. Body weight is the leading one, and it has risen at every survey since 2011.
Judging the epidemic by one survey is like calling the tide by a single wave.
cards.sub: The waterline that decides the next decade, the share of adults overweight, has risen at every reading.

cards.big: A health system can lower a diabetes number for a year. It cannot outrun a population that grows heavier at every survey.
